splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be selected by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability about potential liability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered supervision beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee is a fugitive, so the supervised release term does not run while the person absconds.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory authority to toll supervised release during absconding, and the term expires according to the judgment regardless of the supervisee's fugitive status.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: There is a longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) — which prevents relitigating any previously presented claim — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255, or whether they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s separate requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule. The answer determines whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress may have intended.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners filing § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy the requirements of § 2255(h) for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures such as ballot-counting deadlines. The resolution affects the ability of candidates nationwide to challenge election administration rules before votes are cast, with implications for mail-in voting and ballot counting practices in every state.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates for federal office have Article III standing to challenge state election laws that may affect the counting of votes, including ballot receipt deadlines.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not have sufficient injury-in-fact to establish Article III standing for pre-election challenges to ballot-counting procedures.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/Kisor deference to agency interpretations when construing ambiguous provisions in the federal Sentencing Guidelines that increase criminal penalties.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding criminal sentencing enhancements or instead apply the rule of lenity in favor of defendants when guideline text is ambiguous. At least three circuits hold lenity trumps deference, while the Third Circuit has held deference prevails. The split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over agency deference when construing ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretation.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that Auer/Kisor deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary controls over the rule of lenity when the Commission interprets its own ambiguous Guidelines.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to be met for disqualification) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether a defendant is disqualified from safety-valve relief only if all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) are met, or whether meeting any single criterion suffices. The split creates sentencing disparities nationwide, with defendants having identical criminal histories receiving different sentences depending on their circuit.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' as conjunctive.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines commentary (Application Note 1) expanding the definition of 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy is entitled to deference after Kisor v. Wilkie.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to § 4B1.2, which adds conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the career-offender definition despite their absence from the guideline text, is a valid authoritative interpretation deserving deference or impermissibly expands the guideline beyond its plain meaning. The split affects career-offender sentencing for defendants whose prior convictions are for conspiracy or attempt.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a reasonable interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight under Stinson/Kisor, so inchoate offenses qualify as career-offender predicates.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text — no deference
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition and is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule criminalizes a substance not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the underlying substance to be federally controlled. The Eighth Circuit holds no federal-law limitation should be grafted onto the guideline, while other circuits require the substance to be federally controlled. The split affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with state drug convictions.

positions.label: No federal-law limitation — state schedule suffices
positions.summary: Holds that there is no textual basis to graft a federal-law limitation onto the career-offender guideline; a state drug offense qualifies regardless of whether the substance is federally controlled.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the underlying substance is also controlled under federal law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether non-retroactive changes in law can be considered as part of a compassionate-release analysis. The Eighth Circuit categorically bars consideration of such changes, while other circuits and the Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) permit it. The Seventh Circuit has held the Commission exceeded its authority in the 2024 amendment, deepening the split.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may constitute or contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, consistent with the Sentencing Commission's 2024 policy statement.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be considered
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order denying a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the 30-day civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case itself (subject to the 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but at least one circuit has applied the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is part of the criminal case, so the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's property belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: The Ninth Circuit held that post-petition appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, based on the broad scope of § 541(a). The dissent argued this creates a circuit split with other courts that hold such appreciation belongs to the debtor, consistent with § 348(f)'s purpose of protecting good-faith converters.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, based on the broad definition of 'property of the estate' in § 541(a).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to good-faith converting debtors under § 348(f)(1)(A), which limits the converted estate to property 'as of the date of filing.'

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release at revocation proceedings, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit permits district courts to expressly punish defendants for the conduct underlying supervised release violations, placing it at the extreme end of a circuit split. Other circuits hold that revocation sentences should be calibrated to the breach of trust, not as punishment for the new conduct itself. This affects hundreds of revocation proceedings annually.

positions.label: Punishment for underlying conduct permitted
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants at revocation hearings for the conduct underlying the supervised release violation.

positions.label: Revocation limited to sanctioning breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should reflect the seriousness of the breach of the court's trust, not serve as punishment for the new criminal conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the duration of custody (such as First Step Act time credit denials) must proceed via habeas corpus or may bring civil rights/Bivens claims.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions that affect the fact or duration of confinement, or whether civil rights actions may also be brought. The Eighth Circuit has acknowledged this unresolved conflict persisting for over half a century, implicating challenges to First Step Act time credit decisions.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner challenges the fact or duration of confinement, habeas corpus is the sole federal remedy and civil rights claims are unavailable.

positions.label: Both habeas and civil rights claims available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights claims to challenge conditions or terms of confinement even when habeas may also be available, as long as they are not solely challenging the fact of imprisonment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal medical records in litigation is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as effectively unreviewable after final judgment.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that denials of motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that would be irreparably lost if review were delayed. Other circuits have not recognized such orders as qualifying under the collateral order doctrine, creating a potential split on the scope of immediate appealability for sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal documents referencing medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable because medical privacy is a value of high order that cannot be adequately vindicated on appeal from final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal, including those involving medical records, do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's requirements and must await final judgment for appellate review.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination in the operation of places of public accommodation under Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standards for proving discrimination claims under Title II, including what constitutes differential treatment and what remedies are available. The Supreme Court granted cert to resolve the disagreement, particularly regarding the Second Circuit's approach.

positions.label: Broader standard for proving discrimination
positions.summary: Applies a more plaintiff-friendly standard for proving discrimination in places of public accommodation, requiring less direct evidence of discriminatory intent.

positions.label: Narrower standard for proving discrimination
positions.summary: Applies a more restrictive standard requiring stronger evidence of discriminatory intent in public accommodation discrimination claims.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any ... broker,' preempts common-law negligent hiring and selection claims against freight brokers when the broker arranges transportation that results in injury. The issue affects liability exposure for the freight brokerage industry nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption clause bars state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims relate to the broker's services.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting motor carriers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they are generally applicable safety regulations, not laws related to broker services.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether legal errors or unfairness in a conviction — the type of claims typically raised in habeas petitions under Section 2255 — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar such consideration, while others permit it in certain circumstances.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider claims related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a substitute for habeas relief and courts may not consider legal challenges to the validity of a conviction as extraordinary and compelling reasons.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
